problem_addressed: Standard benchmarks cannot determine how close neural networks are to their theoretical optimum because they lack access to the true posterior p(y|x).
core_claim: Neural networks continue to learn and improve in epistemic uncertainty even when total loss plateaus, and this learning can only be measured with access to exact posteriors.
key_contributions: Using class-conditional normalizing flows as oracles to make exact posteriors tractable on realistic images
Decomposing prediction error into irreducible aleatoric uncertainty and reducible epistemic error
Demonstrating epistemic error follows power laws in dataset size even when total loss plateaus
Showing architectures differ markedly in how they approach the aleatoric floor
Demonstrating training with exact posteriors (soft labels) outperforms hard labels
Enabling exact quantification of distribution shift via KL divergence
Using epistemic uncertainty to distinguish informative samples for active learning
novelty: The use of class-conditional normalizing flows as oracles to make exact posteriors tractable on realistic images, enabling measurement of the true posterior p(y|x) which was previously inaccessible.
method_summary: The authors use class-conditional normalizing flows as oracles to generate exact posterior distributions p(y|x) for realistic images, enabling decomposition of prediction error into aleatoric and epistemic components, and facilitating controlled experiments on distribution shift and active learning.
method_components: Error decomposition into aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty
Power law fitting for epistemic error scaling
KL divergence computation for distribution shift
Epistemic uncertainty-based sample selection for active learning
key_innovation: The use of class-conditional normalizing flows as oracles to make exact posteriors tractable on realistic images, which enables measurement of the true posterior p(y|x) and all subsequent analyses.
assumptions: Class-conditional normalizing flows can accurately model the true posterior p(y|x)
The oracle distributions are representative of real-world data distributions
The decomposition of error into aleatoric and epistemic components is valid
theoretical_grounding: The paper builds on established concepts from Bayesian uncertainty decomposition (aleatoric vs epistemic uncertainty) and neural scaling laws, but provides empirical validation through the oracle framework.
headline_result: Epistemic error follows power-law decay (KL ∝ N^{-α}) across architectures and scales to ImageNet-1000
improvement_over_baseline: ResNets reduce epistemic error by 6× vs. 1.2× for ViTs in low-data regimes
stated_limitations: Uses AFHQ and ImageNet datasets rather than medical images
Computational requirements for normalizing flows may be high
Focuses on classification tasks without explicitly addressing active learning
unstated_weaknesses: Doesn't address domain-specific challenges in medical imaging
Scalability to high-resolution medical images is unclear
Limited connection to active learning strategies
Computational feasibility for medical applications not addressed
threats_to_validity: Generalizability from natural images to medical images is uncertain
Assumption of 'exact' posteriors from normalizing flows may not hold for medical images
Architectural comparisons may not extend to specialized medical imaging architectures
open_questions: How do these findings translate to medical image classification tasks?
Can the exact posterior approach be made computationally feasible for medical imaging?
How can the scaling laws inform active learning strategies for medical images?
What are the aleatoric floors for common medical imaging tasks?
future_work: Extending the work to medical imaging domains
Improving computational efficiency of normalizing flow approaches
Integrating findings with active learning frameworks
gaps_this_enables: Development of calibration-aware active learning strategies for medical imaging
Understanding of fundamental limits in medical image classification
Potential for incorporating soft labels from posteriors in medical image learning
relevance_to_topic: This paper provides foundational insights into calibration and learning limits that could inform calibration-aware active learning for medical image classification.
best_result: Epistemic error follows power-law decay (KL ∝ N^{-α}) across architectures and scales to ImageNet-1000
limitations_stated: Uses AFHQ and ImageNet datasets rather than medical images
Computational requirements for normalizing flows may be high
Focuses on classification tasks without explicitly addressing active learning
title: Beyond the Loss Curve: Scaling Laws, Active Learning, and the Limits of Learning from Exact Posteriors
